planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring or fall, or plant container-grown plants in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, lean to moderately fertile soil in full sun; candytuft blooms on the previous year's growth — shear back by one-third immediately after flowering to keep plants compact and promote dense new growth for next year's bloom. It dislikes hot, humid summer conditions and performs best in cool climates.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; evergreen candytuft is one of the standard spring-blooming edging and rock garden perennials of temperate gardens — a low, spreading, semi-woody subshrub producing dense, flat-topped clusters of brilliant pure-white flowers from March through May that completely cover the plant and provide one of the most reliable and pristine spring displays available. The flowers are produced in such abundance that the dark-green, narrow, glossy, evergreen foliage is invisible beneath the white bloom. After flowering, light shearing maintains compact, dense growth. It is evergreen through winter (retaining its dark-green leaves), adding ground-level interest even when not in flower. Outstanding for edging paths and borders, cascading over low walls, filling rock garden pockets, and providing a spring carpet in full sun. The cultivar 'Snowflake' is a superior form with larger flower heads and particularly dense, rounded flower clusters.

planting.note: Sow seeds in spring or early fall, or plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; shear back hard (by half) immediately after flowering to keep plants compact and prevent them from becoming woody and bare in the center. Aubrieta dislikes hot, humid summer conditions and performs poorly south of zone 7 in the American South.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; purple rock cress is among the most brilliant and cheerful spring-blooming groundcovers of rock gardens and dry stone walls — a low, mat-forming perennial from the eastern Mediterranean and southern Europe producing sheets of vivid violet-purple to lavender to lilac to magenta four-petaled flowers that cascade over walls and edges in March through May in an uninterrupted carpet of color. The named cultivars and hybrids span the full range from pure purple-blue to rose-red and bright magenta, and some have variegated foliage. A classic component of the spring rock garden alongside arabis, iberis, and alyssum, forming a traditional tricolor combination of purple, white, and gold. Outstanding cascading over dry stone walls where its natural habitat — limestone cliff faces of the eastern Mediterranean — is well replicated. Shear back hard after blooming to maintain compact form and prevent the center from going bare.

planting.note: Sow seeds in early spring or fall in well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; shear back by half after flowering to keep plants compact. Arabis caucasica is drought-tolerant and excellent for dry, sunny walls and rock garden situations. It may become invasive in some regions by self-sowing — deadhead to prevent excessive spread.

directions: Plant in well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral, rocky or sandy soil in full sun; Caucasus rock cress is a classic spring rock garden plant producing dense mats of soft, gray-green, woolly-textured leaves and, in April through May, a profusion of pure-white, four-petaled flowers in flat-topped clusters that completely cover the mat and cascade over walls in a clean, fresh display of early-spring white. The grayish, soft-hairy leaf texture is attractive even out of flower, providing a silvery quality in the rock garden year-round. It is among the earliest herbaceous plants to flower in spring and is traditionally combined with purple aubrieta and golden Aurinia saxatilis (basket of gold) to create the classic British spring rock garden color combination of purple, white, and gold. Double-flowered ('Flore Pleno') and pink-flowered ('Pink Pearl') cultivars are available. Variegated-foliage forms have cream-edged leaves and are ornamental year-round.

planting.note: Sow seeds in spring after last frost in lean, well-drained, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun; in zones 9–11 it self-sows as a short-lived perennial. Dahlberg daisy performs best in hot, dry conditions and declines in heat with excessive moisture. Excellent for filling cracks and edges in hot, dry, sunny spots.

directions: Sow in lean, well-drained, sandy to gravelly soil in full sun; Dahlberg daisy is a Texas native annual (perennial in zones 9–11) producing exceptionally fine-textured, thread-like, aromatic foliage — almost needle-like and strongly fragrant when crushed — covered throughout the warm season with masses of tiny, bright golden-yellow daisy flowers, each only 1/2 inch across but produced in such uncountable profusion that the plant appears to be a cushion of living gold. The extremely fine foliage texture combined with the continuous small-scale flower display creates an effect unlike any other — a mounding, golden, aromatic, lacy confection that is outstanding for edging, filling containers, and naturalizing in hot, dry, sandy situations. A Texas Hill Country native, it thrives in the exact difficult conditions — poor, dry, alkaline, rocky soil in baking summer sun — that defeat most garden plants. Self-sows freely to maintain colonies in appropriate climates. The foliage releases a pleasant, chamomile-like herbal fragrance when brushed.

planting.note: Divide established mats or plant small rooted sections in consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in shade; spreading club moss requires constantly moist soil and high humidity — it desiccates rapidly in dry conditions. Excellent for terrariums, woodland floors with consistent moisture, and the margins of streams and ponds in zones 7–10.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full shade; spreading club moss is a South African moss-relative (actually a vascular plant, unrelated to true mosses) that creates a dense, emerald-green to bright-golden-green flat mat of exceptionally fine-textured, scale-like leaves that spreads steadily over moist shaded surfaces — creating a seamless carpet of living green over forest floors, terrarium floors, and other consistently moist, shaded situations. The golden-green cultivar 'Aurea' (gold club moss) is especially popular in terrariums and miniature gardens for its warm, luminous color in deep shade. It spreads by prostrate stems that root at intervals, eventually covering large areas where the consistently moist conditions it requires are maintained. Not drought-tolerant at all — the foliage wilts and browns rapidly if allowed to dry out. An ideal companion to ferns, mosses, and woodland wildflowers in moist, shaded sites with reliable moisture. Also widely used in vivaria and terrariums as a naturalistic ground layer.
